Event Control
Knowledge to Action
• Methods to increase awareness of system events for better system control
• Cyber Security Application
2
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Knowledge Graphs
• Knowledge graphs and graph databases get less attention than Machine Learning, but represent a
powerful AI tool for understanding complex data
• One of the strengths of using a knowledge graph is that you embed the relationships between objects
as primary entities, just like the objects themselves. This allows a graph database to scale with a linear
increase in resource requirements, rather than the exponential increase required by growing relational
databases.
• Querying against millions or billions of objects for secondary or tertiary (and beyond) relationships is
feasible with a graph database. Using relational databases the compute overhead becomes
overwhelming very quickly at scale.
3
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Knowledge Graphs Continued
• Maintaining data as a graph database also opens that data to the tools of graph theory and the many
algorithms that are designed to explore the features of graphs. Many of these tools could be beneficial
in a cyber security context. For example:
‣ Eigenvector centrality and Pagerank can be used to measure the “importance” of a node in a
network and find users who are likely to be used laterally, or find the threat actors and techniques
most likely to have an impact.
‣ Degree can be used to measure the connectedness of a node and its potential impact on other
nodes.
‣ Harmonic closeness centrality can be used to determine what node is able to reach all the other
nodes the most quickly for the deployment of a fix.
4
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Current Knowledge Graph projects
• Neo4J – Current market leader for Graph Database platforms.
• Neptune – Newly announced AWS Graph Database platform that may freeze the market.
• Gephi – Established open source graph analysis platform
• Network models – creating test graph databases using SLM2 network architecture and detailed network
infrastructure data
• Threat actors, tools, and techniques – Applying graph database methods to derive knowledge based on
Mitre’s TTP data.
• Graph representations of customer ticket data – Possible simple first production output using graph
database methods to present ticket-related information to customers.
• Enabling graph queries in SLM widgets – Enable customers to query graph databases through controls in
their own SLM widgets.
5

Example: xxx Routed Networks Autonomous Systems View
6
Model data fed through Yifan Hu algorithm using Gephi. Big dots are xxx Autonomous Systems. Yellow dots are xxx’s. Small red dots represent
the heaviest routes. Each grey circle is an automous network owned by some entity that routes through xxx equipment. Lines represent routes
between autonomous networks. Each node and route is clickable for additional information such as owning company, relative size of route, etc.
25 second loop

Example 4: Threat Actors, Tools, and Techniques
9
This is data from Mitre’s database of threat actors, tools, and techniques. It was converted into a graphic
database to show the connections between the nodes: what actors use what techniques, what applications are
part of which technique family, etc. The graph was then put through an algorithm that adjusted the size of the
node by degree (how many connections it has). Red nodes are threat actors, purple are threat types, pink are
techniques, green are specific applications used.
30 second loop
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Example 5: SLM2 Architecture
10
This data represents the components and data flow of the SLM2 system. The nodes are sized using both
degree and PageRank algorithms to highlight which components have the most connections and which nodes
have connections with the most connections.
30 second loop
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Example 6: Graph Analysis for Anomaly Detection
11
This is a dataset of xxx border routers. Here we can use the Force Atlas 2 algorithm to quick spot data anomalies which
can then be removed to improve training or modeling. The animation on the left shows the anomalous data shoot away
from the main data set and the animation on the right shows a drill down to see what that data is. In this case the
anomalies are column header data that was mistakenly included in the dataset.
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Visualization
• Visualization is a powerful tool for understanding data
• Big, multidimensional data can be rendered in interactive, multidimensional visualizations to create new
understanding and help analysts develop an intuitive “feel” for the data
• Systems should be made to create visualizations that leverage human visual cortex evolution. For
example we are already highly tuned for patterns such as:
‣ Shimmering water
‣ Storm clouds
‣ Movements in grass
‣ Snakes
‣ Facial expressions and symmetries
12
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.

© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Advanced Applications for Combining Graph Databases and
Visualizations
• Topological Data Analysis – finding persistent structures present in multidimensional or time-series data
‣ Helps differentiate meaningful data from noise
‣ Helps show patterns that allow for prediction
‣ Helps clean data for additional AI processing
• The basic idea:
‣ plot the vertex and edge data on a graph and find shapes within that graph (generally triangles).
Simplicial Complexes
‣ Expand the view of the graph out to include more dimensions and see if those shapes persist.
Persistent Homology
‣ The persistence of those shapes in the data across dimensions or time provide evidence of a
structure that conveys some meaningful information about the data
15
© 2019 Joseph Kidd All Rights Reserved.
Advanced Applications for Combining Graph Databases and
Visualizations Continued
• Current visualization techniques rely heavily on dimension reduction to render a stable graph that can
convey meaning.
• The ability to graph shapes across those dimensions, rather than eliminating them, will significantly
improve our ability to see meaningful data structures
• But…
‣ TDA is very computationally heavy
- Imagine possible seating combinations of 10 dinner guests (10!) being about 4m
- 11 dinner guests (11!) about 40m
- 12 dinner guests (12!) about 500m
‣ All of these states need to be stored
‣ The Continuous Variable (CV) quantum algorithm (and others) can be used to make this operation
feasible at scale by “compressing” these states into quantum memory via superposition, then
“coaxing” the answer out back into the classical system.
‣ This will be a good application for quantum computing when the number of qubits a system can
maintain increases.
16
